content.full_text:
Pvi MEHTA
C H A NM AN
FAIR PoLITIcAL
PRACTICES COMMISSION
March 7,
1996
Mr. Darryl Choates
Councilmember,
City of Seaside
1599 Lowell Street
Seaside,
California
93955
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A-96-064
Dear Mr.
Choates:
This is in response to your request for advice regarding the
conflict-of--interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the
“Act”) 1
QUESTION
May you purchase a property located within one of the city’s
redevelopment districts without engendering a conflict of interest
since you are both a city councilmember and the redevelopment
district director?
CONCLUS ION
Yes,
you may purchase property in the city’s redevelopment
district,
but you must disqualify yourself from any decision that
will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on
your economic interest.
FACTS
You are an employee of Seaside Market,
a business owned by
Fairway Mart, Inc., which is located within the redevelopment area
for which you, as a city councilmember for the City of Seaside,
are a director.
Recently, the owners for Seaside Market have
decided to close the store and asked if you were interested in
1
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000—18995.
428 1 STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


File No.
A—96—064
Page
2
buying it.
You are interested in buying the store and in leasing
the property on which the store currently sits for a period of
five years.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from
making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.
Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the. public generally,
on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family or on:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth one
thousand dollars
($1,000.)
or more.
(b)
Any real property in which the public official
has a direct or indirect interest worth one thousand
dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(c)
Any source of income, other than gifts and
other than loans by a commercial lending institution in
the regular course of business on terms available to the
public without regard to official status,
aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars
($250)
or more in value
provided to, received by or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(Section 87103
(a)—(c).)
You stated you have been employed by the Seaside Market,
owned by Fairway Mart, Inc.,
for 18 years.
Assuming the Seaside
Market has paid you more than $250 in the past 12 months, Fairway
Mart, Inc., is a source of income to you.
You also will potentially have two additional economic
interests.
You stated that you plan to acquire a leasehold
interest in the property where the Seaside Market is currently
located.
If you enter into a lease with a duration of more than
30 days,
you will have a real property interest in the form of a
leasehold.
(Section 82033; Regulation 18233.)
In addition, if
you invest more than $1,000 into the Seaside Market,
you will have
an economic interest in the form of a business entity.
(Section
87103(a).)
Thus,
you may not make, participate in making, or use your
official position to influence a governmental decision if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material


File No.
A—96—064
Page
3
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally,
on your source of income.
At this time,
you need only
be concerned about the economic interest you currently hold, the
Fairway Mart,
Inc.,
as a source of income to you.
I have included
in this letter,
however, the regulations and statutes you must
consult in the future if you acquire the leasehold and business
entity interests in the Seaside Market.
Foreseeability
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are
reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case.
An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur.
Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable.
(Downey Cares v.
Downey Community
Development Commission
(1987)
196 Cal.App.3d 938;
In re Thorner
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
198.)
The anticipated result of any redevelopment plan is to
increase the property values and improve the business climate
within the project area.
The very nature of redevelopment
projects has led the Commission to find that it is reasonably
foreseeable that there will be a financial effect on real property
values and business interests located within or near project
areas.
(In re Oglesby
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
71.)
Thus,
since the
Seaside Market is located and does business in the project area,
we presume that a financial effect is foreseeable.
(Eiser Advice
Letter,
No.
A—95—08l.)
Materiality
Regulation 18702 sets forth the guidelines for determining
when the effect of a decision on an official’s economic interest
will be material.
If a government decision will materially affect
of your economic interests,
you will have a conflict of
interest in the decision.
If your economic interest is directly
involved in the decision, the effect is considered material.
(Regulation 18702.1.)
Please consult Regulation 18702.1,
copy
enclosed, to determine if any of your economic interests as
described above are directly involved in any redevelopment
decisions that may come before you on the council.
Source of Income
Where the source of income to an official is a business
entity,
as in this case, Regulation 18702.2 must be applied.
Whether the indirect effect of a decision on a business entity is
material depends on the financial size of the business entity.
Thus, for example, if Fairway Mart, Inc., is a relatively small
business entity, Regulation 18702.2(g)
would apply.
Regulation
18702.2(g)
provides that the effect of a decision is material
where:


File No.
A—96—064
Page
4
(1)
The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year
of $10,000 or more;
or
(2)
The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more; or
(3)
The decision will result in the increase
or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities
of $10,000 or more.
Thus,
you must determine which provision of Regulation
18702.2 applies to the Fairway Mart,
Inc.,
and whether the
financial effect of the decision will reach the thresholds in the
appropriate provision.
If the financial effect meets the
thresholds, the effect on the business is considered to be
material.
Business Entity
Please note,
however,
as we stated above, where any decision
will foreseeably and materially affect
economic interest of
the official in a manner distinguishable from the effect on the
public generally, the official may not participate in the
decision.
At this time,
your only economic interest is your
source of income.
If,
however,
you acquire an investment interest
over $1,000 in the Seaside Market,
you will need to analyze each
decision’s effect on your business entity using Regulation 18702.2
as discussed above.
Leasehold Interest
If you acquire the leasehold interest in the Seaside Market
property,
you will have an economic interest in the farm of a
leasehold situated within the redevelopment area.
If you
determine that it is reasonably foreseeable that the value of this
leasehold interest will be affected by any proposed decisions, the
appropriate standard for determining materiality is that of
Regulation 18702.4 which states that the effect of a decision is
material as to a leasehold interest in real property if any of the
following applies:
(a)
The decision will change the legally
allowable use of the leased property,
and the
lessee has a right to sublease the property;
(b)
It is reasonably foreseeable that the
lessee will change the actual use of the property
as a result of the decision;


File No.
A—96—064
Page
5
(c)
It is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will result in a change in the actual use
of property within 300 feet of the leased property,
and the changed use will significantly enhance or
significantly decrease the use or enjoyment of the
leased property;
(d)
The decision will increase or decrease
the amount of rent for the leased property by $250
or
5 percent, whichever is greater, during any
12—
month period following the decision; or
(e)
The decision will result in a change in
the termination date of the lease.
Accordingly, if you determine that it is reasonably
foreseeable that the value of your leasehold interest will be
affected as described above by any governmental decision,
you
would have to disqualify yourself from participating in such
decisions once you actually acquire the leasehold interest.
“Public Generally” Exception
Regulation 18703 provides an exception to the conflict—of-
interest provisions if the effect on the official’s interest is
not distinguishable from the effect on the public generally.
For
the “public generally” exception to apply,
a decision must affect
the official’s interests in substantially the same manner as it
would affect a significant seqment of the public.
(Regulation
18703.)
Regulation 18703 provides that the “public generally”
exception applies where both subdivisions
(1)
and
(2)
apply.
(1)
significant Segment:
The governmental
decision will affect a “significant segment” of the
public generally as set forth below:
(A)
The decision will affect:
(i)
Ten percent or more of the
population in the jurisdiction of the
official’s agency or the district the
official represents,
or
(ii)
Ten percent or more of all
property owners, all home owners,
or all
households in the jurisdiction of the
official’s agency or the district the
official represents,
or
(iii)
Fifty percent of all
businesses in the jurisdiction or the
district the official represents,
so long


File No.
A—96—064
Page
6
as the segment is composed of persons
other than a single industry, trade,
or
profession; or,
(B)
The decision will affect 5,000
individuals who are residents of the
jurisdiction; or,
***
(2)
Substantially the Same Manner:
The
governmental decision will affect the official’s
economic interest
in substantially the same manner
as it will affect the economic interests of the
segment identified in subdivision
(a) (1)
of this
regulation.
V
As discussed above,
the redevelopment decisions will
necessarily have an impact on the value of property and businesses
in that area.
Regulation 18703 is fact specific and the exception
must be applied on a case-by—case basis.
We do not have any facts
to indicate whether a significant segment of the population of the
city will be affected in the same manner,
however, if you believe
the exception applies and you need further assistance, please feel
free to contact me at
(916)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
V
General Counsel
VV//2
By:
Liane
andolp
Counsel,
Legal Division
SGC:LR: ak
Enclosure

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May you purchase a property located within one of the city’s
redevelopment districts without engendering a conflict of interest
since you are both a city councilmember and the redevelopment
district director?

CONCLUSION: Yes,
you may purchase property in the city’s redevelopment
district,
but you must disqualify yourself from any decision that
will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on
your economic interest.
